Interesting! The RIGHT action moved the blue bar:
- Initial blue: (16,12)-(19,31)
- After RIGHT: (12,24)-(15,43)
So the blue bar moved both right AND up! It went from col 12-31 to col 24-43 (right by 12) and from row 16-19 to row 12-15 (up by 4). This is a diagonal movement.
Wait, but the initial state shows it at (16,12)-(19,31), and after all those UP actions it was at (12,20)-(15,39). So UP actions did move it right (from col 12 to col 20) but not up (it went from row 16 to row 12, but that might have happened in one of the earlier UP actions).
Actually, let me reconsider. The initial state was (16,12)-(19,31). After multiple UP actions, it was at (12,20)-(15,39). So UP moved it both up (16→12) and right (12→20). Now RIGHT moved it from (12,20)-(15,39) to (12,24)-(15,43), which is just right (20→24).
So it seems like:
- UP moves the blue bar up and right
- RIGHT moves the blue bar right
The blue bar is the player! The goal is probably to reach the magenta/charcoal blocks at the top. Let me continue moving right and up to get closer to the target.
Let me check where the magenta/charcoal blocks are and plan a path.

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# RIGHT moved the blue bar! It's the player.

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('c', 'M', 'g', 'w'):  # charcoal, magenta, gray, light gray
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"{n['color']}: ({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)}), pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Also check blue position
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"blue (player): ({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
